text: Hello friends, this is a case of laparoscopic myomectomy for pan-neural myoma, large myoma, which is occupying the entire uterus. And this patient is unmarried girl, so we are challenging, we cannot use uterine manipulator. This is the MRI of the patient and we can see large myoma which is occupying. Now here we will make a baseball diamond concept to put the port and supraumblycal port will be used. that is approximately ten centimeter above the umbilicus because of the size it is important if you go with the telescope in the umbilicus for large myoma you will be in big trouble we cannot perform the surgery because your telescope will touch the umbilicus so this is the camera port that is supra umbilical now we will have seven point five centimeter lateral this is the right hand port so here this is right hand and now again in seven point five centimeter lateral this is the left hand port and now we will put the one contralateral port for miami screw although we will use left hand also miami screw but assistant also can help by miami screw now we will introduce the various needle so with the one ellipsoid number knife

The surgeon creates a supraumbilical incision with a 2mm stab wound on the skin and introduces a Veress needle to establish pneumoperitoneum. The needle is lifted perpendicular to the abdominal wall and oblique to the patient, followed by irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests. Once the preset pressure is reached, the Veress needle is removed, and the incision is enlarged for the primary trocar insertion using a two-hand technique. Diagnostic laparoscopy is performed, and second and third ports are introduced, positioned 7.5 cm lateral and below the primary port, ensuring precise port placement for laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon introduces a 5mm port for the right-hand dissector or harmonic device, positioned 7.5 cm lateral and just above the anterior superior iliac spine. Another port is placed medially for the left-hand myoma screw. The initial diagnostic laparoscopy reveals the uterus occupied by a large myoma, with visible anatomical structures including the uterosacral ligament, fallopian tube, round ligament, infundibulopelvic ligament, ureter, sigmoid colon, cecum, appendix, and various ligaments. The surgeon injects vasopressin to induce transient ischemia, followed by a horizontal elliptical incision on the myoma for easier suturing and removal.

The surgeon uses suction irrigation in the right hand and a myoma screw in the left hand to gradually separate the myoma from the surrounding muscle fibers. A small portion of serosa is sacrificed during the process. Muscle fibers are carefully cut as needed, and the myoma screw is repositioned to optimize traction. The myoma is pushed superiorly, facilitated by the supramolecular port, aiding in its separation. The surgeon avoids using a Maryland grasper for enucleation due to its tendency to break, preferring suction irrigation despite occasional breakage. The myoma is progressively detached, revealing a clear plane of dissection, and is eventually completely removed. The surgeon prepares for four-layer intracorporeal suturing using a 5mm port and needle holder.

The surgeon holds the inner muscular layer of the uterus, preparing it for suturing. The assistant maintains tension on the suture tail to facilitate continuous suturing of this internal muscle layer. No serosa is included in this layer, and the suturing progresses from right to left. After reaching the left corner, suction irrigation is used to clear blood from the area. The suture is then returned to the starting point, forming the second layer of muscle suturing. The suture is terminated using a surgeon's knot or tumble square knot, ensuring it is securely locked. The procedure continues with suturing the zero muscular layer using a Dundee jamming knot technique.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing of the seromuscular layer using a Dundee jamming knot technique. A small tear in the serosa is closed with a starter knot, which self-locks when the needle is pulled through the loop. The suturing progresses horizontally and vertically, approximating the seromuscular layer. Suction irrigation is used to clear blood for better visibility, and the assistant holds the suture as the final layer of serosa is approximated with serous-to-serous suturing.

The surgeon uses the left hand to hold the suture while the right hand performs continuous suturing of the final serous layer. The assistant holds the needle end of the third layer suture, which is not yet tied, as the surgeon reinforces the lower tier. The suturing progresses to the left corner where deep bites are taken to stop oozing, resulting in slight ischemia and paleness at the corner. The needle is cut and removed to facilitate knot tying, and the third layer suture is terminated with the fourth layer suture, completing the secure four-layer closure.

The surgeon removes the suture, confirming no bleeding and a healthy ovary. The process of morselation begins, where the myoma is carefully divided into smaller pieces for removal. The morselated tissue is extracted using a stone holding forcep, ensuring all fragments are cleared. A finger is inserted through the 15 mm port to verify the removal, and a cobbler's needle is used to pass and retrieve the suture. The 15 mm port is closed to prevent hernia.
